text: I'm sharing a case of a perforated appendicitis with a pelvic abscess. This is a young patient who had had about a week worth of discomfort that initially just felt like constipation and then slowly progressed to pain and fevers and presented to an outside center where she got a CAT scan which showed perforated appendicitis with a large pelvic abscess.
She came to my emergency room and in my discussions with her about her options, we elected to go straight to the operating room for appendectomy.

The laparoscopic view from the left upper quadrant port shows loops of small bowel adherent to the bladder and anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon carefully attempts to separate these adhesions to access the collection deep to the bladder.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers side to side, gently dissecting to avoid injury to the small bowel. The appendix is visible on the right of the screen, plastered beneath the bladder. The surgeon enters the abscess cavity, takes cultures, and performs suction and irrigation.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers to unroof the abscess cavity, aiming to remove the small bowel from the pelvis. The abscess is located between the bladder and the uterus, and the surgeon works to clear the small bowel from these areas to fully expose the cavity.

The surgeon carefully uses blunt dissection to separate inflammatory adhesions, revealing the terminal ileum being lifted from the right pelvic side wall. The cavity, as indicated by the CAT scan, is visible beneath the bladder and anterior to the uterus, contained within this specific area.
The surgeon performs irrigation in the pelvic cavity, ensuring there are no residual fluids or debris beneath the uterus and in the deep pelvis near the rectum.

The surgeon targets the appendix, which is adherent to the terminal ileum. Using gentle dissection, they separate inflammatory adhesions, focusing on the anti-mesenteric fat of the terminal ileum that is stuck to the appendix. The surgeon decides to proceed through the mesoappendix to facilitate separation.

The surgeon encounters difficulty visualizing the base of the appendix due to adhesions. They are dissecting towards the base, attempting to separate the appendix from the anti-mesenteric fat of the terminal ileum and the ileum itself. Some separation is beginning to occur.

The surgeon uses a ligature to perform finger fracture technique, successfully separating the appendix from the adherent anti-mesenteric fat and terminal ileum. The appendix is then placed into a retrieval bag for removal.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the staple line and the abscess cavity, followed by additional irrigation. The surgical field shows localized containment within the cavity, eliminating the need for a drain.
The surgeon fills the cavity with omentum, aiming to introduce healthy tissue to promote healing and encourage fibrinous exudate formation.
